narrative:
Let's take a moment to understand the prediction made by the model regarding your bar exam outcome. Currently, the model predicts that you might not pass the bar exam on your first attempt, which led to the rejection of your university application. This prediction is based on several factors from your academic and personal background, such as your grades, LSAT score, and whether you plan to work full-time while studying.

To help you understand what could change this prediction, we use something called counterfactual scenarios. These are "what if" situations that show how altering certain aspects of your profile could lead to a different outcome, specifically passing the bar exam. By examining these scenarios, we can identify which changes are most impactful.

In your case, the model suggests that improving your undergraduate GPA and switching from full-time to part-time work are key factors that could change the prediction. Across the scenarios, your GPA consistently needed to increase from 2.8 to at least 3.1, and working part-time instead of full-time was another common change. Interestingly, some features like your LSAT score and family income did not need to change in these scenarios, indicating they were less critical in this context.

We generated several counterfactual scenarios, and the most frequent changes involved your GPA and work status. These two features often changed together, suggesting that improving your academic performance while reducing work commitments could be an effective strategy.

Based on these insights, you might consider focusing on boosting your GPA by approximately 0.3 points. This could involve seeking additional academic support or dedicating more time to your studies. Additionally, if feasible, transitioning to part-time work could allow you more time to focus on your studies, potentially improving your academic outcomes. By making these adjustments, you could significantly increase your chances of passing the bar exam and having your application approved. Remember, these are suggestions based on patterns observed in the data, and your personal circumstances should guide any decisions you make.
